video_title: Lawfare Daily: What Do the Russians Actually Think About the War?
gist: CSIS's Maria Snegovaya argues Russian war-support polling is broadly reliable and splits supporters into a hardcore 'hawk' minority (15-30%) and a larger, status-quo 'loyalist' majority (35-50%) whose passive support hinges on personal economic well-being rather than ideology; Ukraine's refinery strikes and the resulting gasoline shortages are eroding loyalist support faster than battlefield casualties ever did, but a real breaking point likely requires a full-blown economic crisis.

insights.claim: Methodological studies using techniques like list experiments (which let respondents mask sensitive views) have found no major systematic bias in Russian war-support polling since 2022; discrepancies top out around 10 percentage points in the most skeptical studies and zero in others.
insights.speaker: Maria Snegovaya, CSIS senior fellow
insights.so_what: Investors and analysts tracking Russian sentiment as a war-termination signal can treat headline polling numbers as directionally trustworthy rather than dismissing them as propaganda artifacts.
insights.quote: There are no major biases in the Russian polling.

insights.claim: Passive alignment with Russia's war has fluctuated between roughly 70-80% earlier in the conflict and has eroded into the mid-60s in recent years, even after adjusting for an estimated 10 percentage points of respondents who may misrepresent their true views.
insights.speaker: Maria Snegovaya, CSIS senior fellow
insights.so_what: A multi-year downward trend in even the most conservative reading of war support is a slow-moving but trackable indicator of Kremlin political capacity.

insights.claim: Extreme Scan, an independent (non-state-funded) Russian pollster run by sociologist Elena Koneva who has since been designated a 'foreign agent' by the Russian state, focuses on borderline regions like Belgorod and Kursk near the front line, making it the best available proxy for how ordinary Russians react to direct war exposure such as drone strikes on refineries.
insights.speaker: Maria Snegovaya, CSIS senior fellow
insights.so_what: Extreme Scan's border-region data is the closest available real-time gauge of Russian reaction to Ukraine's deep-strike campaign and is worth monitoring as a leading indicator.

insights.claim: Russian war supporters split into two structurally different groups: ideological 'hawks' (roughly 15-30% of the population, depending on how national identity strength is measured) and status-quo 'loyalists' (roughly 35-50%), and this heterogeneity explains why the same population can simultaneously show majority war support and majority desire to end the war.
insights.speaker: Maria Snegovaya, CSIS senior fellow
insights.so_what: Because loyalists are the larger group and their support is conditional on personal well-being rather than ideology, they are the segment most likely to swing quickly if economic conditions worsen — a key variable for forecasting Kremlin political durability.

insights.claim: Hawks skew male, from smaller towns and villages, consume state TV propaganda heavily, hold revanchist/neo-imperialist views, and historically believed Putin was too weak internationally — until he starts a war (this is his fifth), at which point they rally behind him as having 'finally' acted correctly.
insights.speaker: Maria Snegovaya, CSIS senior fellow
insights.so_what: This group ('Z-niks') will not be moved by economic pressure or negotiated settlements that don't include territorial gains, making them a persistent hard-line constituency regardless of war outcome.

insights.claim: Loyalists skew female, are politically disengaged, consume more diverse media, and support the war passively only insofar as it doesn't disrupt their personal lives — they delegate all judgment about the war's justification to Putin ('Putin does the right thing... I delegate these decisions').
insights.speaker: Maria Snegovaya, CSIS senior fellow
insights.so_what: This delegation-of-agency dynamic means loyalist 'support' is really a green light contingent on personal comfort, so any shock that disrupts daily life (economic decline, fuel shortages) has outsized power to flip this majority segment.
insights.quote: Putin does the right thing. If he decided to do it, he knows what he's done.

insights.claim: Moscow has shifted from a relative opposition stronghold (site of the 2012 Bolotnaya protests) to a pro-Putin, pro-war city, driven partly by administrative redistricting that annexed new provincial territory into Moscow and partly because many Muscovites have economically benefited from the war and Western sanctions.
insights.speaker: Maria Snegovaya, CSIS senior fellow
insights.so_what: Moscow's political character can no longer be assumed to be liberal or anti-war, which matters for any analysis using regional variation to gauge Kremlin vulnerability.
insights.quote: Moscow, unfortunately, has become very pro-Putin and pro-war.

insights.claim: Since 2011-12 the Kremlin has deliberately replaced independent entrepreneurial and business jobs with state and public-sector positions, engineering an electorate that is economically dependent on — and therefore more politically loyal to — the state.
insights.speaker: Maria Snegovaya, CSIS senior fellow
insights.so_what: This is a structural, multi-decade policy that makes the loyalist bloc more durable than a simple wartime propaganda effect would suggest — economic dependency, not just messaging, underpins Kremlin support.

insights.claim: Economic shocks are far more corrosive to loyalist war support than military or battlefield shocks; hawks rally around the flag regardless of cost, while loyalists' support is conditioned almost entirely on their perceived personal economic well-being.
insights.speaker: Maria Snegovaya, CSIS senior fellow
insights.so_what: For anyone modeling Kremlin political risk, economic indicators (real incomes, fuel availability, inflation) are more predictive of eroding war support than casualty counts or territorial setbacks.

insights.claim: In the war's first two years, Kremlin 'military Keynesianism' — large fiscal stimulus into the military-industrial complex — raised working-class incomes and reduced inequality, which paradoxically increased war support; that dynamic has now reversed as the Russian economy stagnates and some analysts flag recession risk since early 2026.
insights.speaker: Maria Snegovaya, CSIS senior fellow
insights.so_what: The economic tailwind that initially bought the Kremlin political support from war spending has expired, and the same mechanism is now working in reverse against Putin.

insights.claim: Since early 2026, Russia's economy has been stagnating with some analysts suggesting outright recession, coinciding with measurable erosion of war support and growing public demand for peace talks.
insights.speaker: Maria Snegovaya, CSIS senior fellow
insights.so_what: A stagnating-to-recessionary Russian economy is a concrete macro signal correlated with declining Kremlin political capacity to sustain the war.

themes.theme: Refinery strikes and the gasoline shortage as leverage

insights.claim: Ukrainian drone strikes on Russian refineries have produced gasoline shortages and lines reminiscent of the late Soviet period, a shared, universally felt experience that is proving uniquely effective at eroding loyalist war support — unlike battlefield casualties, which nearly everyone across the political spectrum has tolerated.
insights.speaker: Maria Snegovaya, CSIS senior fellow
insights.so_what: Refinery strikes function as an economic-pressure lever specifically targeted at the loyalist majority's decision-relevant variable (personal well-being), making them potentially more effective at shifting Russian opinion than frontline attrition.

insights.claim: For three consecutive months starting in May, Russians have reported Ukrainian drone strikes as the most significant event of the month, a trend that directly coincides with an ongoing decline in war approval.
insights.speaker: Maria Snegovaya, CSIS senior fellow
insights.so_what: A sustained, self-reported salience of drone strikes in Russian polling is a trackable leading indicator for continued erosion in Kremlin war support.

insights.claim: A poll by the Institute of Conflictology and Analysis of Russia (ECAR) found a record 81% of respondents want the war to end — the first time such a high figure has been recorded — though this is not a clean anti-war signal since most of those same respondents still want Russia to keep the Ukrainian territory it currently occupies.
insights.speaker: Maria Snegovaya, CSIS senior fellow
insights.so_what: Headline 'want the war to end' numbers should not be read as support for a fair or unconditional settlement; any peace-talk framing needs to account for entrenched territorial demands even among war-weary Russians.
insights.quote: a record 81% of respondents say that they want the war to end

insights.claim: Reduced refined-fuel output from strikes hits two channels at once: it lowers loyalist public morale via civilian gasoline shortages, and it directly constrains the Russian military's own fuel supply, giving Ukraine's refinery campaign a dual civilian-opinion and battlefield-logistics payoff.
insights.speaker: Maria Snegovaya, CSIS senior fellow
insights.so_what: Ukraine's refinery-strike strategy should be evaluated as a combined economic-warfare and public-opinion tool, not just a battlefield-logistics play — it compounds pressure on the Kremlin from two directions simultaneously.

themes.theme: Casualty tolerance and the elusive breaking point

insights.claim: Russian tolerance for battlefield casualties has proven far higher than analysts expected; a RAND Corporation study found that what matters to Russians is believing the war is morally justified and winnable, not minimizing human losses — respondents didn't especially value even a hypothetical replacement of soldiers with robots to reduce casualties.
insights.speaker: Maria Snegovaya, CSIS senior fellow
insights.so_what: Strategies premised on casualty attrition alone breaking Russian political will are likely to underdeliver; economic and normalization-of-suffering dynamics matter more than body counts.
insights.quote: We have discovered that the price of human life in Russia is extremely low.

insights.claim: A genuine breaking point for Russian war support would likely require a full-blown economic crisis on the scale of 2008 or the late-Soviet collapse, and any such shift is expected to be gradual rather than sudden, given the Kremlin's decades of experience managing public sentiment.
insights.speaker: Maria Snegovaya, CSIS senior fellow
insights.so_what: Investors and policymakers betting on a near-term Russian capitulation should recalibrate toward a longer, grinding timeline pegged to macro deterioration rather than a single triggering event.
insights.quote: I think the real breaking point and then again that's pure speculation.

insights.claim: In a separate study with Graham Robertson and Peter Pomerantsev, priming Russians on national identity ('Russianness') measurably reduced their willingness to support immediate peace negotiations, indicating a durable revanchist constituency will persist even if Putin himself pushes for peace talks.
insights.speaker: Maria Snegovaya, CSIS senior fellow
insights.so_what: Any negotiated settlement risks facing a domestic identity-driven backlash constituency in Russia regardless of what Putin agrees to, complicating assumptions that a Kremlin deal automatically translates into durable Russian public buy-in.

themes.theme: Regime response and the limits of loyalist discontent

insights.claim: The Kremlin actively polls and manages public perception — for example, running pro-Kremlin ad campaigns during the 2022 partial mobilization to downplay how many people were being called up — because it knows public sentiment constrains its options even without competitive elections as a formal check.
insights.speaker: Maria Snegovaya, CSIS senior fellow
insights.so_what: Public opinion still functions as an informal resource constraint on Kremlin war-fighting capacity, meaning opinion erosion has real operational consequences even absent electoral accountability.

insights.claim: Political science research shows public dissatisfaction in autocratic regimes is more likely to manifest as elite splits than as street protests, which is the more probable channel through which eroding Russian loyalist sentiment could eventually threaten Kremlin stability.
insights.speaker: Maria Snegovaya, CSIS senior fellow
insights.so_what: Analysts should watch for signs of elite defection or infighting rather than mass demonstrations as the leading indicator of regime stress from declining war support.
insights.quote: public dissatisfaction is more likely in autocratic regimes to lead to elite splits

insights.claim: The 2022 partial mobilization triggered a mass exodus of Russians fleeing conscription rather than organized protest, illustrating that avoidance and exit — not confrontation — is the characteristic Russian response to unpopular state action, echoing late-Soviet-era sabotage and disengagement.
insights.speaker: Maria Snegovaya, CSIS senior fellow
insights.so_what: Expect continued passive resistance (draft evasion, emigration, quiet non-compliance) rather than mass protest even as loyalist discontent grows, which still degrades Kremlin war capacity without producing visible political crisis.

insights.claim: No single official justification for the war — protecting Russian speakers in Ukraine or stopping NATO expansion among them — has ever gathered majority support among Russians, showing that even 4.5 years in, there is no consensus domestic rationale for the war.
insights.speaker: Maria Snegovaya, CSIS senior fellow
insights.so_what: The absence of a unifying domestic justification is a latent vulnerability the Kremlin has to keep managing through propaganda and normalization rather than genuine persuasion, and it underpins why economic disruption (not ideology) is the more effective lever on loyalists.
